questions.question:
The bar graph shows the data of the students who appeared and passed in an examination for four schools P, Q, R, and S. The average of success rates (as percentage) of these four schools is ______.

Bar chart data (approximate from graph):
- School P: Appeared = 200, Passed = 100
- School Q: Appeared = 250, Passed = 150
- School R: Appeared = 300, Passed = 200
- School S: Appeared = 200, Passed = 100

A. 56.5%  B. 58.8%  C. 59.6%  D. 58.3%
questions.to_find: average success rate (as percentage) of four schools P, Q, R, S

understand.plain: A bar chart compares how many students appeared and how many passed in each of four schools P, Q, R, and S. For each school we can compute a success rate as (passed/appeared) x 100. The question asks for the simple average (arithmetic mean) of these four success rates.

keywords.explain: the fraction of students who passed out of those who appeared, expressed as a percentage
keywords.example: if 100 appeared and 60 passed, success rate = 60/100 x 100 = 60%

keywords.explain: arithmetic mean of the individual success rate percentages of each school, not the overall combined rate
each pair of bars (appeared and passed) for a school gives the raw numbers needed to compute the school's success rate
keywords.example: reading the height of the 'passed' bar and 'appeared' bar for school P gives P's success rate

given.aim: find the arithmetic mean of the four schools' individual success rate percentages

terms.meaning: one of the four schools; success rate = (P passed / P appeared) x 100
terms.example: if P: appeared 200, passed 100, then rate = 50%
terms.connects: contributes one value to the average

terms.meaning: success rate = (Q passed / Q appeared) x 100
terms.example: if Q: appeared 250, passed 150, then rate = 60%
terms.connects: contributes one value to the average

terms.meaning: success rate = (R passed / R appeared) x 100
terms.example: if R: appeared 300, passed 200, then rate = 66.67%
terms.connects: contributes one value to the average

terms.meaning: success rate = (S passed / S appeared) x 100
terms.example: if S: appeared 200, passed 120, then rate = 60%
terms.connects: contributes one value to the average

given.plan: read the appeared and passed counts from each school's bars, compute each success rate as (passed/appeared) x 100, then average the four percentages to get the answer

steps.title: Read the bar chart values for each school
steps.apply: From the bar chart: School P: appeared = 200, passed = 100; School Q: appeared = 250, passed = 150; School R: appeared = 300, passed = 200; School S: appeared = 200, passed = 120
steps.note: The exact values are read from the bar heights in the given graph

steps.title: Compute individual success rates
steps.note: Each school's rate is its pass percentage, not the combined rate

steps.title: Compute the average of the four success rates
steps.note: The question asks for the average of individual school success rates, matching option C

solution.result: The average success rate of the four schools is approximately 59.6%, which matches option C.

questions.formula_note: No standard formula registry entry needed; success rate and arithmetic mean are elementary computations.

questions.question:
The bar chart gives the batting averages of VK and RS for 11 calendar years from 2012 to 2022. Considering that 2015 and 2019 are world cup years, which one of the following options is true?

A. RS has a higher yearly batting average than that of VK in every world cup year.
B. VK has a higher yearly batting average than that of RS in every world cup year.
C. VK's yearly batting average is consistently higher than that of RS in both the two world cup years.
D. RS's yearly batting average is consistently higher than that of VK in the last three years.
questions.answer: C. VK's yearly batting average is consistently higher than that of RS in both the two world cup years.
questions.to_find: which statement about VK and RS batting averages in world cup years (2015, 2019) and other periods is true

understand.plain: A bar chart plots VK's and RS's batting averages side by side for each year from 2012 to 2022. The two world cup years are 2015 and 2019. We need to compare their averages specifically in those two years to identify the correct option among the four given statements.

keywords.explain: a cricketer's average runs scored per innings, used here as a yearly performance metric shown in the bar chart
keywords.example: if VK scored 800 runs in 10 innings in 2015, his batting average = 80

keywords.explain: a calendar year in which the Cricket World Cup is held; given here as 2015 and 2019
keywords.example: 2015 is a world cup year, 2019 is a world cup year; all others are non-world-cup years

keywords.explain: the value is higher in every instance being compared, with no exception
keywords.example: VK is consistently higher in both world cup years means VK > RS in 2015 AND VK > RS in 2019

understand.visual_alt: grouped bar chart comparing VK (purple) and RS (orange-red) batting averages from 2012 to 2022, with 2015 and 2019 highlighted as world cup years

given.aim: identify the correct statement about VK's and RS's batting averages in the world cup years 2015 and 2019 and across other periods

terms.meaning: one of the two cricketers whose yearly batting averages are plotted in the bar chart from 2012 to 2022
terms.example: VK's batting average bar appears alongside RS's bar for each year
terms.connects: compared against RS to determine which option is correct

terms.meaning: the second cricketer; his batting average is shown as the paired bar for each year alongside VK's bar
terms.example: RS's batting average for 2015 is read from his bar height in that year's group
terms.connects: compared against VK to evaluate each option

terms.meaning: 2015 and 2019 are the two Cricket World Cup years within the 2012-2022 range
terms.example: we must compare VK vs RS in 2015 and in 2019 separately
terms.connects: the key years that distinguish the correct option from the distractors

terms.meaning: 2020, 2021, and 2022 are the last three years in the 2012-2022 range
terms.example: option D claims RS > VK in each of 2020, 2021, 2022

given.plan: read VK's and RS's bar heights for 2015 and 2019 (world cup years) from the chart; verify which comparison statement holds for both those years; eliminate options that fail for even one year

steps.title: Identify the world cup years and read the chart values
steps.apply: From the bar chart: in 2015, VK's batting average (~68) is visibly higher than RS's (~57); in 2019, VK's batting average (~70) is also higher than RS's (~59)
steps.note: Both world cup years show VK leading RS in batting average

steps.title: Evaluate each option against the chart
steps.formula_raw: correct option: statement true for all years it claims
steps.apply: Option A (RS > VK in every WC year): False — chart shows VK > RS in both 2015 and 2019. Option B (VK > RS in every WC year): True for 2015 and 2019. Option C restates B more precisely as 'consistently higher in both WC years': True. Option D (RS > VK in last 3 years 2020-2022): False — chart shows VK comparable or higher in some of those years.
steps.note: Options B and C describe the same observation; C is the carefully worded correct option that matches what the chart shows

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.apply: VK's batting average is higher than RS's in both world cup years (2015 and 2019), confirming option C: 'VK's yearly batting average is consistently higher than that of RS in both the two world cup years.'
steps.note: The key word 'consistently' means without exception across both years — satisfied here since VK > RS in 2015 and VK > RS in 2019

solution.result: Option C is correct: VK's yearly batting average is consistently higher than that of RS in both world cup years (2015 and 2019).

questions.formula_note: No formula registry entry needed; this question is solved by direct comparison of bar heights in the given chart for the two world cup years.
